beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: I love that the router maps observe, intervene, and would-have keyword families onto Pearl's three rungs, but you flagged that 'does advertising cause sales?' has no intervention keyword yet clearly belongs on Rung Two. I want to fix that blind spot. Help me: (1) build a labeled set of a dozen causal questions where the rung is not signalled by any obvious keyword; (2) replace the brittle keyword table with a Claude classification call and compare the two on that set; (3) log which rung each disagrees on and why.

props.command: I love that the router maps observe, intervene, and would-have keyword families onto Pearl's three rungs, but you flagged that 'does advertising cause sales?' has no intervention keyword yet clearly belongs on Rung Two. I want to fix that blind spot. Help me: (1) build a labeled set of a dozen causal questions where the rung is not signalled by any obvious keyword; (2) replace the brittle keyword table with a Claude classification call and compare the two on that set; (3) log which rung each disagrees on and why.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: When you ask an LLM a causal question, it cannot tell you which rung of the ladder it is answering from. Pearl's Ladder of Causation has three rungs: Rung One, observe — what do we see? Rung Two, intervene — what happens if we act? Rung Three, counterfact — what would have happened if we had acted differently? Most LLMs conflate all three. A causal router makes the rung explicit before answering.
beats.visual_intent: title card: Pearl's Ladder — Rung 1 (observe), Rung 2 (intervene), Rung 3 (counterfact); question routing diagram

beats.narration_text: Into the terminal: claude — build a Python causal query router CLI: classify a natural-language question as Rung One, Two, or Three; route to the appropriate response strategy — Rung One gives a statistical answer, Rung Two requires a DAG and structural assumptions, Rung Three requires a counterfactual world specification.

Read it. The classification logic maps keyword families to rungs: observe, correlate, and show keywords map to Rung One; change, intervene, and set-to keywords map to Rung Two; would have, had, and if-only keywords map to Rung Three. The routing then gates the response on what the rung requires.

props.code:
# causal_router.py — Claude Code output
def classify_rung(question: str) -> int:
    q = question.lower()
    # Rung 3: counterfactual
    if any(w in q for w in ['would have','had we','if only','what if we had']):
        return 3
    # Rung 2: intervention
    if any(w in q for w in ['if we','what happens if','do(','set to','intervene']):
        return 2
    # Rung 1: observational
    return 1

rung = classify_rung(question)  # Rung 1: observe, 2: intervene, 3: counterfact

beats.narration_text: Run it on three test questions. What is the correlation between price and sales — Rung One, statistical. What happens if we lower the price — Rung Two, requires a DAG. Would sales have been higher if we had lowered the price last quarter — Rung Three, requires a counterfactual world. The router nails all three.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal showing three questions classified and routed correctly to their respective rung response strategies

beats.narration_text: The keyword matching is brittle for Rung Two — the question 'does advertising cause sales?' should be Rung Two, but it contains no intervention keywords. Iterate: use Claude to classify ambiguous questions before falling back to keyword rules.

props.command:
claude "update causal_router.py:
  -> for ambiguous questions use Claude API to classify
  -> fallback to keyword rules for clear cases
  -> test: 'does advertising cause sales?'"

beats.narration_text: Re-run. The causal question 'does advertising cause sales?' now routes to Rung Two — the LLM recognizes causation as an intervention claim, not a correlation. Ambiguous questions are no longer misclassified.
beats.visual_intent: screen-recording or slate: terminal showing improved routing with Claude API disambiguation

beats.narration_text: The ladder is not a metaphor. It is a formal distinction: Rung One answers need only data, Rung Two answers need a causal model, Rung Three answers need a model and a counterfactual world. A router that makes the rung explicit before answering stops LLMs from giving Rung One answers to Rung Three questions.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: three rungs with their data requirements; 'explicit rung -> appropriate response' stated plainly

beats.narration_text: Your move: build a test set of twenty causal questions across all three rungs and run the router against it. When it misclassifies, look at the failure mode — is it a Rung Two question masquerading as Rung One, or a Rung Three question without the 'would have' marker? Each failure type has a different fix.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) build 20-question test set across all rungs, 2) analyze misclassification failure modes, 3) distinguish Rung 2/3 masquerade patterns

A router that makes the rung explicit before answering stops LLMs from giving Rung One answers to Rung Three questions. beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Causal Inference — Causal Query Router] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
